chapters.title: Mapping & Memory
chapters.desc: Grid-stride loops, multidimensional indexing, broadcasts and coalesced data movement.

chapters.title: Reductions & Scans
chapters.desc: Cooperative collectives that turn many values into summaries, prefixes and compact layouts.

chapters.title: Atomics & Ordering
chapters.desc: Contention, stable rearrangement and the building blocks behind GPU sorting.

chapters.title: Matrices & Neighborhoods
chapters.desc: Tiling, reuse, boundary handling and arithmetic intensity in structured workloads.

chapters.title: Sparse & Irregular Workloads
chapters.desc: Variable work, indirect memory access, graph traversal and scientific simulation.

chapters.title: Elementwise Ops
chapters.desc: One thread per element. Learn the grid-stride basics and memory bandwidth.

chapters.title: Embeddings
chapters.desc: Token + positional encodings: gathers on the way in, scatter-adds on the way out.

chapters.title: Reductions & Normalization
chapters.desc: Softmax, LayerNorm, norms. Shared memory, warp shuffles, online algorithms.

chapters.title: Matrix Multiplication
chapters.desc: The op that dominates training time. Tiling, permutes and exploiting structure.

chapters.title: Attention
chapters.desc: The heart of the transformer. Compose everything you learned so far.

chapters.title: Loss & Classifier
chapters.desc: Cross-entropy over a 50k vocabulary, where fusion pays off the most.

chapters.title: Fusion & Optimizer
chapters.desc: Fused blocks and the AdamW update: the last pieces of the training loop.

problems.title: Grid-Stride SAXPY
problems.summary: Scale one vector and accumulate it into another across arbitrary input sizes.
statement: Implement SAXPY in place: `y[i] = a * x[i] + y[i]` for every element.
Use a grid-stride loop so the same launch handles inputs much larger than the physical thread grid.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Update y in place without modifying x
constraints: x and y contain finite float32 values
The launch must guard all out-of-range indices
problems.note: SAXPY is a standard memory-bandwidth exercise. Grid-stride loops decouple correctness from a particular grid size.

problems.title: Fused Affine ReLU
problems.summary: Fuse an affine transform and ReLU into one memory pass.
statement: Compute `out[i] = max(scale * x[i] + bias, 0)` for every element.
Fusion avoids writing the intermediate affine result to global memory.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Store one result for every input element
constraints: All inputs and scalars are finite float32 values
problems.note: Fusing cheap elementwise operations usually saves more time in memory traffic than it costs in arithmetic.

problems.title: Row Broadcast Add
problems.summary: Broadcast a row vector across every row of a matrix.
statement: For a row-major matrix, compute `out[r,c] = matrix[r,c] + row[c]`.
Map a flat thread index back to the column while keeping matrix accesses coalesced.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Do not modify either input
constraints: Inputs are contiguous row-major float32 arrays
problems.note: Broadcasting appears throughout tensor programs and rewards simple flat indexing with coalesced access.

problems.title: Gather
problems.summary: Read values through an index array into a dense output.
statement: Compute `out[i] = src[indices[i]]` for `N` requested positions.
The output writes are coalesced even though source reads may be irregular.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Preserve index order in the output
problems.note: Gather is the basic indexed-read primitive behind embeddings and sparse workloads.

problems.title: Collision-Free Scatter
problems.summary: Place values into unique indexed output positions.
statement: Write `out[indices[i]] = values[i]` into a zero-initialized output of length M.
Indices are unique, so no atomics are needed in this first scatter exercise.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Leave output positions not named by indices equal to zero
problems.note: Removing the uniqueness guarantee turns this into an atomic accumulation problem later in the curriculum.

problems.title: Matrix Transpose
problems.summary: Transpose a row-major matrix while reasoning about memory coalescing.
statement: Given an H by W row-major matrix, write its W by H transpose so `out[c,r] = inp[r,c]`.
A correct direct mapping is simple; a tiled shared-memory version makes both reads and writes coalesced.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Handle rectangular and non-tile-aligned matrices
constraints: Input and output do not alias
problems.note: Transpose isolates coalescing and shared-memory bank-conflict lessons without hiding them behind heavy arithmetic.

problems.title: Sum Reduction
problems.summary: Reduce an arbitrary-length float vector to one sum.
statement: Compute the sum of all N input values and store it in `out[0]`.
Correct implementations must handle non-power-of-two sizes; optimized versions combine block reduction with a final device-wide stage.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Accumulate in float32
constraints: Floating-point addition order may differ from the reference
problems.note: Reduction introduces shared memory, synchronization, warp shuffles and multi-stage device-wide cooperation.

problems.title: Dot Product
problems.summary: Fuse pairwise multiplication with a device-wide sum.
statement: Compute `out[0] = sum(a[i] * b[i])` for N float elements.
Do not materialize the products: multiply during the reduction.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Accumulate products in float32
problems.note: The dot product is the smallest useful example of fusing a map operation into a reduction.

problems.title: Row-Wise Sum
problems.summary: Produce one reduction result per matrix row.
statement: For an R by C row-major matrix, compute one sum for every row.
Choose a mapping that gives each row cooperative threads and works when C is not a power of two.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Write exactly R output values
problems.note: Row reductions are a stepping stone to normalization and batched collective kernels.

problems.title: Argmax Reduction
problems.summary: Reduce values and indices together with deterministic tie breaking.
statement: Return the maximum input value and its index.
When the maximum occurs more than once, return the lowest index. A reduction state therefore carries both a float and an integer.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Use the lowest index on exact ties
constraints: The maximum is copied exactly from the input
problems.note: Pair reductions are common in selection, sparse processing and diagnostics.

problems.title: Inclusive Prefix Sum
problems.summary: Compute a device-wide inclusive scan for arbitrary input sizes.
statement: Compute `out[i] = inp[0] + ... + inp[i]` for every i.
The contract is device-wide, so inputs may span many blocks and have non-power-of-two lengths.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Preserve input order in every prefix
problems.note: Scan is the allocation and rearrangement primitive behind compaction, radix sorting and sparse construction.

problems.title: Segmented Sum
problems.summary: Reduce variable-length segments described by an offset array.
statement: For segment s, sum `values[offsets[s]:offsets[s+1]]`.
Offsets are nondecreasing, so repeated offsets represent valid empty segments whose sum is zero.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Write one output for every segment including empty ones
constraints: Offsets are nondecreasing and values are finite
problems.note: Segmented reduction models sparse rows, ragged batches and reduce-by-key workloads.

problems.title: Stream Compaction
problems.summary: Stably keep positive values and return a variable-length result.
statement: Copy every input value greater than zero to a dense output while preserving original order.
Write the retained length to `count[0]`; only `out[0:count]` is judged.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
The output order must be stable
constraints: The valid output count may be zero
problems.note: The standard parallel solution maps predicates to flags, scans the flags and scatters selected values to their prefix positions.

problems.title: Integer Histogram
problems.summary: Count integer categories under both uniform and highly skewed input distributions.
statement: Count N integer values in `[0,B)` into a zero-initialized array of B bins.
Correctness requires atomic updates; optimized versions privatize bins per block before merging.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Every input contributes to exactly one bin
problems.note: Skewed histograms expose atomic contention that uniform random benchmarks can hide.

problems.title: Scatter Add
problems.summary: Accumulate values into indexed destinations with collisions.
statement: For every input, perform `out[indices[i]] += values[i]` into a zero-initialized output.
Many inputs may name the same destination, so plain loads and stores create a data race.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Accumulate every colliding contribution
problems.note: Scatter-add is the write-side counterpart of gather and appears in embeddings, graphs and sparse gradients.

problems.title: Run-Length Encoding
problems.summary: Encode adjacent equal values as unique values and run counts.
statement: Split the input into maximal adjacent runs, then emit each run value and length in order.
Store the number of runs in `runs[0]`; only the valid prefixes of unique and counts are judged.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Preserve adjacent run order exactly
problems.note: Parallel RLE detects boundaries, scans boundary flags and derives each run length from neighboring starts.

problems.title: Block Bitonic Sort
problems.summary: Sort one power-of-two integer array cooperatively within a block.
statement: Sort N signed int32 values in ascending order, where N is a power of two no larger than 1024.
Use the compare-exchange stages of a bitonic sorting network with synchronization between dependent stages.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Return all values in nondecreasing order
constraints: N is a power of two
Duplicates and negative values are allowed
problems.note: Bitonic sort trades extra comparisons for a regular, synchronization-friendly control structure.

problems.title: Radix Digit Pass
problems.summary: Stably reorder key-value pairs by one eight-bit radix digit.
statement: Sort key-value pairs by the unsigned byte `(uint32(keys[i]) >> shift) & 255`, preserving input order for equal digits.
The shift is one of 0, 8, 16 or 24; signed keys are treated as raw 32-bit patterns for digit extraction.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
The digit pass must be stable
constraints: Keys and payload values are signed int32
problems.note: A stable digit pass combines histograms, prefix offsets and stable scattering into the core step of radix sort.

problems.title: Full Radix Sort
problems.summary: Compose stable digit passes into a full key-value integer sort.
statement: Stably sort nonnegative int32 keys in ascending order while applying the same permutation to values.
Handle arbitrary N by processing every key byte and ping-ponging intermediate buffers as needed.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Equal keys must keep their original value order
constraints: Values are arbitrary signed int32 payloads
problems.note: A complete radix sorter exercises histograms, scans, stable scatter and multi-pass buffer management together.

problems.title: Top-K Selection
problems.summary: Return the largest K values and their original indices in deterministic order.
statement: Select K input elements and emit them by descending value.
Equal values are ordered by their lower source index, and the output indices always refer to the original input.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Return exactly K value-index pairs in the specified order
constraints: Lower indices win exact value ties
problems.note: Top-K rewards hierarchical selection that avoids sorting elements which cannot enter the result.

problems.title: Matrix-Vector Multiply
problems.summary: Multiply a dense row-major matrix by a vector.
statement: Compute `out[r] = sum(matrix[r,c] * x[c])` for every row.
Each output is an independent dot product; cooperate within rows while keeping matrix reads contiguous.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Do not modify the matrix or vector
problems.note: Matrix-vector multiply has low reuse compared with matrix multiplication and is often memory-bandwidth limited.

problems.title: Outer Product
problems.summary: Construct a matrix from every pair of two vector elements.
statement: For vectors a of length M and b of length N, compute `out[i,j] = a[i] * b[j]`.
Map the two-dimensional output so consecutive threads write consecutive columns.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Write a contiguous row-major M by N output
constraints: Input and output buffers do not alias
problems.note: Outer products are simple two-dimensional maps and form rank-one matrix updates.

problems.title: RGB to Grayscale
problems.summary: Convert interleaved RGB pixels to luminance values.
statement: For each HWC pixel, compute `gray = 0.299*R + 0.587*G + 0.114*B`.
The RGB input is interleaved, so each pixel occupies three consecutive float values.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Use the coefficients exactly as specified
constraints: Input layout is contiguous H by W by 3
problems.note: Image layout exercises make indexing errors visible while retaining simple independent pixel work.

problems.title: 1D Convolution
problems.summary: Apply an odd-width kernel with same-size zero-padded output.
statement: Compute one-dimensional cross-correlation with kernel center `K/2` and zero values outside `[0,N)`.
Each output reuses neighboring inputs; shared-memory tiles can cache the interior and halo.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Do not reverse the kernel coefficients
constraints: Inputs and kernel are finite float32
problems.note: The shared-memory version introduces halo loading and synchronization around neighborhood data reuse.

problems.title: 2D Five-Point Stencil
problems.summary: Apply a five-point discrete Laplacian over a 2D grid.
statement: For each cell compute `4*center - up - down - left - right`.
Neighbors outside the H by W domain are zero, including at corners and edges.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Produce an output for every grid cell
problems.note: A tiled stencil cooperatively loads an interior tile plus boundary halos into shared memory.

problems.title: 2D Convolution
problems.summary: Apply a small 2D kernel with zero padding and same-size output.
statement: Compute 2D cross-correlation using an odd K by K kernel centered over every input pixel.
Treat samples outside the H by W domain as zero and do not reverse the kernel.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Handle image sizes that are not multiples of the tile dimensions
constraints: Inputs and kernel are finite float32
problems.note: The optimized kernel cooperatively loads a shared-memory tile with a two-dimensional halo and reuses each input sample.

problems.title: CSR Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiply
problems.summary: Multiply a CSR sparse matrix by a dense vector across uneven row lengths.
statement: For each row r, sum `values[j] * x[col_idx[j]]` over `j` in `[row_ptr[r],row_ptr[r+1])`.
Rows may be empty or highly unbalanced, creating irregular memory access and variable work.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Write zero for empty rows
constraints: CSR offsets are valid and column indices are in [0,C)
problems.note: CSR SpMV is usually limited by indirect memory traffic and demands load balancing when row lengths vary.

problems.title: COO to CSR
problems.summary: Canonicalize unsorted coordinate entries into CSR row storage.
statement: Convert unsorted `(row,column,value)` entries into CSR row offsets, columns and values.
Order entries by row, then column, then original input position so duplicate coordinates remain deterministic.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Emit canonical stable ordering and R+1 row offsets
constraints: Coordinates are in bounds and duplicate coordinates are allowed
problems.note: The conversion combines key ordering, row histograms, prefix offsets and deterministic sparse scattering.

problems.title: Breadth-First Search
problems.summary: Compute directed unweighted shortest paths from one source in a CSR graph.
statement: Return the minimum hop distance from source to every vertex, or -1 when no directed path exists.
The graph may contain cycles, self-loops and duplicate edges; a parallel solution repeatedly expands a frontier while claiming unseen vertices safely.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Set distance[source]=0 and preserve -1 for unreachable vertices
constraints: CSR offsets and destination vertices are valid
problems.note: BFS exposes frontier construction, irregular expansion, duplicate discovery and atomic visited-state transitions.

problems.title: N-Body Acceleration
problems.summary: Accumulate softened gravitational acceleration for every pair of 3D bodies.
statement: For body i, sum contributions from every j other than i using `mass[j] * (position[j]-position[i]) / (distance_squared + softening^2)^(3/2)`.
Use G=1 and write one three-component acceleration per body.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Exclude self interaction and use the supplied softening value
constraints: Positions and masses are finite float32 and masses are positive
softening is finite and positive in judge tests
problems.note: Tiling positions and masses in shared memory turns an all-pairs simulation into a classic data-reuse exercise.

problems.title: Residual Forward
problems.summary: Element-wise addition of two tensors: the residual (skip) connection.
statement: Write a GPU kernel that performs element-wise addition of two vectors of 32-bit floats: `out[i] = inp1[i] + inp2[i]`.
In a transformer, this is the residual (skip) connection applied after attention and after the MLP. It is trivially parallel: launch one thread per element and guard against out-of-bounds indices.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
The final result must be stored in out
constraints: This kernel is memory-bound: 2 reads + 1 write per element
problems.note: Applied twice per transformer block in the full training loop. A common optimization uses 128-bit packed loads to maximize bandwidth.

problems.title: GELU Forward
problems.summary: The GELU activation (tanh approximation) used in the GPT-2 MLP.
statement: Implement the GELU activation function using the tanh approximation, applied element-wise:

$$
\mathrm{GELU}(x) = 0.5\,x\left(1 + \tanh\!\left(\sqrt{2/\pi}\,(x + 0.044715\,x^3)\right)\right)
$$
This is the non-linearity between the two matmuls of every transformer MLP block. Like all element-wise ops it is memory-bound: the tanh is cheap compared to the loads and stores.
requirements: Use the tanh approximation exactly as given (this is what GPT-2 uses)
The solve function signature must remain unchanged
Results must match the CPU reference within 1e-5
constraints: N = B × T × 4C (the MLP hidden dim is 4×768 = 3072 in GPT-2 124M)
Use tanhf, not tanh: stay in fp32
problems.note: The naive version assigns one element per thread; an optimized version processes 8 elements per thread via 128-bit vectorized loads for full memory bandwidth.

problems.title: GELU Backward
problems.summary: Backpropagate through the GELU: chain rule on an element-wise op.
statement: Given the original input `inp` and the upstream gradient `dout`, compute the gradient with respect to the input: $\mathrm{dinp}_i = \mathrm{dout}_i \cdot \mathrm{GELU}'(\mathrm{inp}_i)$.
Differentiating the tanh approximation gives, with $k = \sqrt{2/\pi}$ and $u = k\,(x + 0.044715\,x^3)$:
where $\mathrm{sech}^2(u) = 1 - \tanh^2(u)$. Element-wise backward passes have the same shape as their forward pass: one thread per element.
requirements: External libraries are not permitted
dinp must be written, inp and dout are read-only
Results must match the CPU reference within 1e-5

examples.output: dinp = [1.0830]  // local gradient of GELU at x=1

constraints: Do not recompute the forward output; only the local gradient is needed
3 reads + 1 write per element: still memory-bound
problems.note: Production training code stores the pre-activation inp during the forward pass precisely so this kernel can be a cheap element-wise op instead of recomputation.

problems.title: Encoder Forward
problems.summary: Token embedding + positional embedding: the first op of the network.
statement: The encoder turns a batch of token ids into the model's first activations. Given token ids `inp` of shape (B, T), a token embedding table `wte` of shape (V, C) and a positional embedding table `wpe` of shape (T, C), compute:

$$
\mathrm{out}[b, t, c] = \mathrm{wte}[\mathrm{inp}[b, t],\, c] + \mathrm{wpe}[t, c]
$$
This is a gather (indexed read) followed by an add. Think about which parallelization is best: one thread per (b, t) that loops over C, or one thread per (b, t, c)?
requirements: The solve function signature must remain unchanged
out has shape (B, T, C), laid out contiguously
inp contains valid token ids in [0, V)
constraints: Memory-coalesced access matters: consecutive threads should read consecutive floats
problems.note: Switching from one-thread-per-(b,t) to one-thread-per-(b,t,c) yields a ~4× speedup from better parallelism and coalescing.

problems.title: Encoder Backward
problems.summary: Scatter-add gradients into the embedding tables: your first race condition.
statement: Backpropagate through the encoder. Given upstream gradients `dout` of shape (B, T, C), accumulate into the embedding gradients:
`dwte[inp[b, t], c] += dout[b, t, c]` and `dwpe[t, c] += dout[b, t, c]`

The catch: the same token can appear at many (b, t) positions, so multiple threads will write to the same row of `dwte` concurrently. A plain `+=` is a data race. Use `atomicAdd`, or find a smarter decomposition.
requirements: dwte and dwpe are pre-zeroed; accumulate into them
The result must be deterministic up to floating-point addition order
No external libraries

examples.output:
dwte[5] = [3.0]   // both positions hit token 5
dwpe = [[1.0], [2.0]]

constraints: Token distribution is skewed: common tokens create heavy atomic contention
problems.note: A smarter decomposition splits the work so dwpe needs no atomics at all (parallelize over (t, c), loop over b). Only dwte truly needs atomicAdd.

problems.title: Softmax Forward
problems.summary: Numerically-stable row softmax with shared-memory reductions.
statement: Compute the softmax of each row of an (N, C) matrix:

Subtracting the row max is not optional: without it, the exponential overflows for logits above ~88. Each row needs two reductions (max, then sum). Assign one block per row and cooperate through shared memory, or go further with warp shuffle intrinsics.
Bonus: the online softmax algorithm computes max and sum in a single pass by rescaling the running sum whenever a new max appears.
requirements: Must be numerically stable (subtract the row max)
One kernel launch; the solve signature must remain unchanged
Each output row must sum to 1.0 within 1e-5
constraints: N = B × NH × T rows, C = T columns (attention) or C = V (logits)
C can be as large as 50,257; a single thread per row is far too slow
problems.note: Production implementations of this kernel evolve through many versions: naive, shared-memory blocks, then warp-per-row online softmax. The online variant is what ships inside attention in real training loops.

problems.title: LayerNorm Forward
problems.summary: Normalize each row to zero mean / unit variance, then scale and shift.
statement: Implement LayerNorm over the channel dimension. For each of the $N = B \times T$ rows of `inp` (each of length C):
with $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$. Additionally write $\mu$ into `mean[i]` and $1/\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \varepsilon}$ into `rstd[i]`: the backward pass needs them and recomputation is wasteful.
Like softmax this is a row-wise reduction: one block (or one warp) per row is the natural mapping.
requirements: Write all three outputs: out, mean, rstd
Use ε = 1e-5 inside the square root
weight and bias have length C and are shared across rows
constraints: Var(x) computed as E[x²] − E[x]² is one pass but numerically riskier; both are accepted here
Aim for a single kernel launch
problems.note: Optimized implementations build from a naive per-row thread to cooperative-groups warp-per-row. LayerNorm appears twice per transformer block: 24 times in GPT-2 124M.

problems.title: Global Gradient Norm
problems.summary: Reduce 124M floats to a single number: the L2 norm for gradient clipping.
statement: Compute the sum of squares of an entire tensor: $\mathrm{out} = \sum_i \mathrm{data}_i^2$. (The host takes the square root to get the L2 norm used for gradient clipping.)

This is a full-array reduction to one float. A single block cannot span the array, so use the classic two-stage pattern: each block reduces its slice into a partial result, then a second kernel (or atomics) combines the partials.
Watch out for the naive trap: having every thread `atomicAdd` into one float serializes the whole GPU.
requirements: out is a single float on the device, pre-zeroed
You may launch more than one kernel from solve
Result must match the CPU sum within relative 1e-2 (fp32 addition order differs)
constraints: At most one atomicAdd per block, not per thread
Grid-stride loops keep the block count reasonable
problems.note: The fastest versions use a fixed grid sized to the GPU (blocks × SMs), a grid-stride loop, warp-level reduction, and one atomic per block. Gradient clipping runs this every training step.

problems.title: LayerNorm Backward
problems.summary: Three gradients at once (dinp, dweight, dbias) with cross-row accumulation.
statement: Backpropagate through LayerNorm. Given `dout` (B,T,C), the forward input `inp`, `weight`, and the cached `mean` and `rstd`, compute:

$$
\mathrm{dinp}_{b,t,c} = \mathrm{rstd} \cdot \left(\mathrm{dnorm} - \mathrm{mean}(\mathrm{dnorm}) - \mathrm{norm} \cdot \mathrm{mean}(\mathrm{dnorm} \cdot \mathrm{norm})\right)
$$
where $\mathrm{norm} = (\mathrm{inp} - \mu) \cdot \mathrm{rstd}$ and $\mathrm{dnorm} = \mathrm{weight} \cdot \mathrm{dout}$.

The difficulty: `dinp` reduces within a row, but `dweight`/`dbias` reduce across all rows. Two conflicting parallelization axes in one kernel. This is widely considered the hardest non-attention kernel in a GPT-2 training loop.
requirements: All three gradient outputs must be written; dweight/dbias are pre-zeroed and accumulated
Use the cached mean and rstd; do not recompute them
Tolerance 1e-3 vs the CPU reference (accumulation order differs)
constraints: Naive atomicAdd on dweight/dbias for every (b,t,c) will be extremely slow
Consider per-block scratch buffers + a final reduce kernel
problems.note: The fastest known implementations use warp-per-row for dinp, block-local shared-memory accumulators for dweight/dbias, and a tiny second kernel to fold the scratch buffer.

problems.title: Matmul Forward
problems.summary: out = inp · Wᵀ + bias. 90%+ of training FLOPs live here.
statement: Implement the linear layer. Treating `inp` as a $(B \cdot T, C)$ matrix and `weight` as $(OC, C)$ (PyTorch layout: one output channel per row), compute:

A naive one-thread-per-output-element kernel gets ~1% of peak FLOPs. The classic fix is shared-memory tiling: each block loads a tile of `inp` and a tile of `weight` into shared memory, and every thread computes a small register tile of outputs.
You will not beat cuBLAS; the point is to understand what cuBLAS is doing for you.
requirements: No cuBLAS / CUTLASS: write the tiles yourself
bias may be NULL; handle that case
Tolerance 1e-1 absolute vs CPU reference (fp32 accumulation at this scale)
constraints: Arithmetic intensity is O(tile size): bigger tiles = more compute per byte
Watch shared memory bank conflicts on the weight tile
problems.note: A hand-tiled 128×128 blocktile kernel reaches a respectable fraction of cuBLAS. Production training loops call cuBLAS for this op, but you should know why.

problems.title: Matmul Backward Bias
problems.summary: Column-wise reduction: dbias[o] = Σ over all rows of dout[·, o].
statement: Compute the bias gradient of a linear layer: $\mathrm{dbias}_o = \sum_{b,t} \mathrm{dout}_{b,t,o}$.

It looks trivial (it is just a column sum) but doing it fast is subtle. `dout` is row-major, so a thread that walks down one column makes strided, uncoalesced reads. The trick is to have each warp read contiguous rows and reduce across the row dimension while keeping full-width coalesced loads.
This deceptively simple kernel is notorious for needing many optimization iterations to get fast.
requirements: dbias is pre-zeroed; accumulate into it
Reads from dout must be coalesced for full credit (benchmark honesty!)
Tolerance 1e-3
constraints: A one-thread-per-column kernel is ~30× slower than the best version
Shared memory or warp shuffles for the cross-row reduction
problems.note: The optimization ladder for this kernel is a masterclass in reduction tuning: thread-per-column → warp-per-column → block-per-column-group with fp32 scratch accumulation.

problems.title: QKV Permute
problems.summary: Reshape the fused QKV projection into head-major Q, K, V tensors.
statement: The attention block computes Q, K, V with a single fused matmul, producing `inp` of shape (B, T, 3, NH, HS): for each position, Q then K then V, split into NH heads of size HS.

Attention math wants each tensor head-major: (B, NH, T, HS), so that one attention head's data is contiguous. Write a kernel that scatters `inp` into three output tensors `q`, `k`, `v` with that layout.
Pure index arithmetic, no math. The skill being tested is deriving flat offsets for multi-dimensional layouts without off-by-one errors.
requirements: One elementwise pass; each input float is read exactly once
Derive the index math from the shapes; no helper libraries
q, k, v each have B·NH·T·HS elements
constraints: Writes should be coalesced: parallelize over the output index
problems.note: Permute kernels like this appear throughout the forward and backward attention passes: layout transforms are the connective tissue of the attention block.

problems.title: Triangular Matmul
problems.summary: Q·Kᵀ, but skip everything above the diagonal: causality is free FLOPs.
statement: Causal attention only ever looks backwards: position $t$ attends to positions $0 \dots t$. That means the score matrix $\mathrm{preatt} = QK^\top / \sqrt{HS}$ is lower-triangular; the upper half is masked away and computing it is pure waste.
Write a batched matmul over (B, NH) head pairs that computes only the lower triangle:

$$
\mathrm{preatt}[b, h, t_1, t_2] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{HS}} \sum_i Q[b, h, t_1, i] \cdot K[b, h, t_2, i] \qquad \text{for } t_2 \le t_1
$$

The interesting part is the block decomposition: diagonal tiles are half-full, sub-diagonal tiles are dense, super-diagonal tiles can be skipped entirely. Done right, this halves the FLOPs of the biggest non-matmul op in the model.
requirements: Entries above the diagonal must be left untouched (or set to -INFINITY, document which)
Skip (do not just mask) the work above the diagonal
No cuBLAS batched-gemm calls
constraints: Target: ≥ 1.6× the runtime of the equivalent dense batched matmul
Tile the T×T plane; classify tiles as skip / dense / diagonal
problems.note: The classic solution is a blocktile kernel that enumerates only the lower-triangular tiles. Same idea as FlashAttention's causal-block skipping.

problems.title: Attention Forward
problems.summary: The full multi-head causal self-attention pipeline.
statement: Implement multi-head causal self-attention for GPT-2. Given the permuted tensors `q`, `k`, `v` of shape (B, NH, T, HS):
**1.** Scores: $\mathrm{preatt}[b, h, t_1, t_2] = \sum_i q[b, h, t_1, i] \cdot k[b, h, t_2, i] \,/\, \sqrt{HS}$, for $t_2 \le t_1$
**2.** Causal softmax: $\mathrm{att}[b, h, t_1, \cdot] = \mathrm{softmax}(\mathrm{preatt}[b, h, t_1, 0 \dots t_1])$; future positions get exactly 0
**3.** Weighted sum: $\mathrm{out}[b, h, t_1, i] = \sum_{t_2 \le t_1} \mathrm{att}[b, h, t_1, t_2] \cdot v[b, h, t_2, i]$
You may use three kernels (matmul-like, softmax, matmul-like); that is how reference implementations work. Fusing them is how you reinvent FlashAttention.
requirements: Causality is exact: att[t1, t2] = 0 for t2 > t1
The softmax must be numerically stable
Multiple kernel launches from solve are allowed
constraints: preatt and att are (B,NH,T,T) scratch, 1.5 GB at B=32; this is why FlashAttention exists
problems.note: Real implementations progress from the naive 3-kernel pipeline through fused-softmax variants to cuDNN FlashAttention. The softmax uses one warp per row with the online max/sum trick.

problems.title: Attention Backward
problems.summary: The boss fight: backprop through scores, causal softmax, and values.
statement: Backpropagate through the attention forward pass. Given `dout` and the saved forward tensors (`q`, `k`, `v`, `att`), produce `dq`, `dk`, `dv`.
Chain rule through three stages, in reverse:
**1.** Through the weighted sum: $\mathrm{datt} = \mathrm{dout} \cdot v^\top$ and $\mathrm{dv} = \mathrm{att}^\top \cdot \mathrm{dout}$

**2.** Through the causal softmax: $\mathrm{dpreatt}[t_1, t_2] = \mathrm{att}[t_1, t_2] \cdot \left(\mathrm{datt}[t_1, t_2] - \sum_{t_3} \mathrm{datt}[t_1, t_3] \cdot \mathrm{att}[t_1, t_3]\right) / \sqrt{HS}$
**3.** Through the scores: $\mathrm{dq} = \mathrm{dpreatt} \cdot k$ and $\mathrm{dk} = \mathrm{dpreatt}^\top \cdot q$
The softmax-backward (stage 2) is the performance-critical piece; production implementations iterate through many versions of it alone.
requirements: All of dq, dk, dv must be written (pre-zeroed, accumulate where convenient)
Respect causality everywhere: t2 > t1 contributes nothing
Tolerance 1e-2 vs CPU reference

examples.input:
T=1, HS=1: single position
q=[[1]], k=[[2]], v=[[3]], att=[[1]]
dout = [[1]]
examples.output:
dv = [[1]]   // att is identity at T=1
dq = [[0]], dk = [[0]]  // softmax of one element is constant

constraints: The softmax Jacobian is dense per-row; reduce before you scatter
problems.note: The optimization ladder for the softmax backward runs from one-thread-per-element with atomics to a block-per-row formulation with no atomics at all: a short course in GPU optimization.

problems.title: Cross-Entropy Forward
problems.summary: losses[b,t] = −log(probs[target]): a gather and a log.
statement: Given softmax probabilities `probs` of shape (B, T, V) and integer targets of shape (B, T), compute the per-token loss:

$$
\mathrm{losses}[b, t] = -\log\left(\mathrm{probs}[b, t, \mathrm{targets}[b, t]]\right)
$$
Each output element touches exactly one input float: parallelize over the $B \cdot T$ positions. The subtlety is purely about indexing into the huge (B, T, V) tensor correctly.
requirements: One thread per (b, t) position is sufficient
Use logf (fp32)
The solve signature must remain unchanged
constraints: probs rows already sum to 1; no normalization needed here
problems.note: The mean of losses over all (b,t) is the number you watch go down during training. In optimized training loops this kernel is eventually absorbed into the fused classifier.

problems.title: Cross-Entropy + Softmax Backward
problems.summary: The famous simplification: dlogits = probs − one_hot(target).
statement: Backpropagating through softmax followed by cross-entropy loss collapses into one beautiful expression:

$$
\mathrm{dlogits}[b, t, v] = \left(\mathrm{probs}[b, t, v] - \mathbb{1}\{v = \mathrm{targets}[b, t]\}\right) \cdot \mathrm{dlosses}[b, t]
$$
No Jacobians, no reductions: the two gradients cancel into a subtraction. Implement it with one thread per (b, t, v) element.
If you have ever wondered why every framework fuses softmax with cross-entropy: this identity is why.
requirements: dlogits is pre-zeroed; write (or accumulate) every element
Exact indicator: subtract 1.0 only at the target index
Tolerance 1e-5
constraints: B·T·V elements ≈ 1.6B at max size; bandwidth is everything
problems.note: The naive version is nearly optimal already: the op is a pure streaming pass. The real win (next problem) is never materializing probs at all.

problems.title: Fused Classifier
problems.summary: Softmax + loss + gradient in one kernel; never materialize the probs.
statement: The classifier layer works on (B, T, V) logits with V = 50257. Running softmax-forward, crossentropy-forward, and crossentropy-softmax-backward as separate kernels streams that 1.6B-element tensor through global memory three times.
Fuse all three into a single kernel. For each (b, t) row of logits:
**1.** Online-softmax reduction: one pass over the row computing the running max and rescaled exp-sum
**2.** $\mathrm{losses}[b, t] = -\left(\mathrm{logit}[\mathrm{target}] - \mathrm{max} - \log(\mathrm{sumexp})\right)$
**3.** Second pass: overwrite the row in place with $\mathrm{dlogits} = \left(\mathrm{softmax}(\mathrm{logit}) - \mathbb{1}\{\mathrm{target}\}\right) \cdot \mathrm{dloss}$
The `probs` tensor is never written to memory. This single fusion is one of the largest speedups in the entire training loop.
requirements: One kernel launch; one block (or warp group) per (b, t) row
logits may be overwritten in place with dlogits
Use the online softmax algorithm: two passes over the row maximum
constraints: V does not fit in shared memory; stream it with a grid-stride row loop
problems.note: The fastest versions add 128-bit vectorized loads on top of the fusion. Deleting the probs tensor also saves 6.4 GB of memory at B=32.

problems.title: Fused Residual + LayerNorm
problems.summary: Two ops, one pass: add the residual and normalize while it is still in registers.
statement: In every transformer block, a residual add is immediately followed by a LayerNorm. Run separately, the intermediate tensor makes a full round-trip to global memory just to be re-read.
Fuse them: for each row, compute `residual = inp1 + inp2`, keep it in registers/shared memory, and immediately produce `normed = layernorm(residual)` along with the cached `mean` and `rstd`.
Both `residual` and `normed` must still be written out (the backward pass needs the residual), but the fused version reads the inputs once instead of twice.
requirements: Single kernel launch producing all four outputs: residual, normed, mean, rstd
ε = 1e-5; same LayerNorm semantics as the earlier problem
Tolerance 1e-5
constraints: One block (or warp) per row; the row fits comfortably in shared memory
Target: ≥ 1.5× faster than running the two kernels separately
problems.note: Optimized versions escalate from simple fusion to warp-per-row with 128-bit vectorized loads. Production training code uses this fusion for every one of the 2×12 layernorms in GPT-2 124M.

problems.title: AdamW Optimizer Step
problems.summary: The update rule that actually trains the model: moments, bias correction, decay.
statement: Implement one AdamW update step over all parameters. For each parameter $i$, with gradient $g$:

$$
m_i = \beta_1 m_i + (1 - \beta_1)\,g \qquad v_i = \beta_2 v_i + (1 - \beta_2)\,g^2
$$

$$
\mathrm{params}_i \leftarrow \mathrm{params}_i - \mathrm{lr} \cdot \left(\frac{\hat{m}}{\sqrt{\hat{v}} + \varepsilon} + \mathrm{weight\_decay} \cdot \mathrm{params}_i\right)
$$

Element-wise over 124M parameters, but with four tensors in flight (`params`, `grads`, `m`, `v`): a good exercise in register pressure and read-modify-write bandwidth. The bias-correction terms $(1 - \beta^t)$ are the same for every element: compute them on the host.
requirements: m, v, and params are all updated in place
Pass bias corrections in as beta1_correction / beta2_correction; do not call powf per thread
Use the decoupled weight decay (AdamW), not L2-regularized Adam
constraints: num_parameters up to 124,000,000 (use long for the index!)
4 reads + 3 writes per element: pure bandwidth
β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e-8, lr=3e-4, wd=0.1 are typical GPT-2 training defaults
problems.note: A key optimization moves the bias-correction divides out of the inner loop. This kernel runs once per training step over every parameter; at 124M params it is not free.
